does: This paper proposes GARDO, a framework for fine-tuning diffusion models via reinforcement learning that mitigates reward hacking while maintaining sample efficiency and exploration capability. The core contribution is a gated and adaptive regularization mechanism that selectively applies policy regularization based on the divergence between proxy reward signals and a diversity-aware objective, rather than applying uniform regularization against a reference policy. The method is evaluated on text-to-image generation tasks where it demonstrates improved alignment with human preferences while preserving generation diversity and enabling effective exploration of high-reward regions that the original model cannot reach.
connects: This work addresses a fundamental challenge in the RL fine-tuning of generative models identified in recent literature: the gap between proxy rewards used for optimization and actual task performance. It builds on prior work in RL-based image generation that showed online RL can improve text-to-image alignment but revealed the reward hacking problem where metrics improve while perceptual quality degrades. The paper extends standard policy regularization approaches from the broader RL literature, which commonly use KL divergence constraints to prevent distribution shift, but innovates by making regularization adaptive rather than uniform, drawing on insights about when and where regularization helps versus hinders learning.
enables: GARDO's selective regularization approach opens pathways for more effective RL-based optimization of other generative models where ground-truth objectives are difficult to specify precisely, including video generation, 3D object generation, and code generation tasks. The framework's compatibility with various RL algorithms makes it a general tool that could be integrated into emerging fine-tuning methods. By solving the reward hacking problem more efficiently than prior approaches, this work enables larger-scale exploration of high-quality, high-diversity generated outputs and could facilitate better alignment of generative models with complex, multi-faceted human preferences without the traditional exploration-exploitation trade-offs.
